concepts.lesson: Matter is made up of particles that are incredibly small—beyond our imagination. These particles are not continuous (like a block of wood) but particulate in nature. This is evidenced by the fact that when we dissolve salt in water, the salt particles occupy the spaces between water particles.
concepts.real_example: Dissolving a crystal of potassium permanganate in a large volume of water colors the whole solution, showing that one crystal is made of millions of tiny particles.

concepts.lesson: Particles of matter are continuously moving and possess kinetic energy. As temperature rises, particles move faster. They also have a force of attraction acting between them which keeps them together. This force varies in strength from one substance to another (strongest in solids, weakest in gases).
concepts.real_example: The smell of hot sizzling food reaches you several meters away because the kinetic energy of the particles is high due to heat.

concepts.lesson: Solids have a definite shape, distinct boundaries, and fixed volumes. They have negligible compressibility and tend to maintain their shape when subjected to outside force. They are rigid because their particles are closely packed with very strong forces of attraction.
concepts.real_example: An iron nail cannot be compressed or easily deformed compared to a sponge.

concepts.lesson: Liquids have no fixed shape but have a fixed volume. They take up the shape of the container in which they are kept. Liquids flow and change shape, so they are not rigid but are called fluids. The space between particles is greater than in solids, allowing for diffusion.
concepts.real_example: Water poured from a bottle into a bowl changes its shape but the volume remains the same.

concepts.lesson: Gases have neither a fixed shape nor a fixed volume. They are highly compressible compared to solids and liquids. Because of high speed of particles and large space between them, gases show the property of diffusing very fast into other gases.
concepts.real_example: LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas) cylinders use the high compressibility of gas to store large volumes in a small space.

concepts.lesson: On increasing the temperature of solids, the kinetic energy of the particles increases, causing them to vibrate with greater speed. At a certain point (melting point), the particles break free from their fixed positions and the solid converts to a liquid. During this process, the temperature remains constant as heat is used to overcome the forces of attraction; this is called Latent Heat.
concepts.real_example: A mixture of ice and water stays at 0°C until all the ice has melted, despite continuous heating.

concepts.lesson: Sublimation is a change of state directly from solid to gas without changing into liquid state. The reverse process, changing from gas directly to solid without becoming liquid, is called deposition.
concepts.real_example: Camphor or ammonium chloride disappears when heated, turning directly into vapor.

concepts.lesson: Applying pressure and reducing temperature can liquefy gases. By bringing the particles closer together, the state of matter can be changed. For example, solid carbon dioxide (dry ice) is stored under high pressure and sublimes directly to gas when pressure is decreased to 1 atmosphere.
concepts.real_example: Solid CO2 is called 'dry ice' because it doesn't turn into liquid when pressure is released.
key_points: Gases can be liquefied by pressure
State depends on both P and T

concepts.lesson: Evaporation is a surface phenomenon where particles from the surface gain enough energy to overcome the forces of attraction and change into vapor at any temperature below the boiling point. It is influenced by surface area, temperature, humidity, and wind speed.
concepts.real_example: Wet clothes dry faster on a windy day or when spread out (increased surface area).

concepts.lesson: During evaporation, the particles of liquid absorb energy from the surroundings to regain the energy lost during evaporation. This absorption of energy from the surroundings makes the surroundings cold. This is the principle behind many natural and artificial cooling systems.
concepts.real_example: People sprinkle water on the roof or open ground in summer because the large latent heat of vaporisation of water helps to cool the hot surface.
key_points: Latent heat of vaporisation is key
